close_reason: Superseded by forkhub-hgm (Wire SyncService → Analyzer). Same root cause, more complete description with fix plan and verification steps. Part of the forkhub-7qk analyzer decoupling epic.
description:
The sync pipeline in src/forkhub/services/sync.py discovers forks and populates vitality/stars but has no code path that invokes AnalyzerService or AnalysisRunner. As a result:

1. New forks are inserted without running provider.compare() (sync.py:225-240) — commits_ahead/commits_behind stay at 0
2. AnalyzerService exists in src/forkhub/services/analyzer.py as dead code — it is not imported or called from sync.py, cli/sync_cmd.py, or anywhere else
3. The analysis pass (coordinator + diff-analyst subagent) that generates Signal rows is never triggered by 'forkhub sync'

Impact: The signals table stays empty regardless of how many forks are discovered. The backfill feature (candidates/apply/etc) has nothing to operate on because signals never get produced.

Discovered while smoke-testing the v0.3.0 backfill primitives against a newly-tracked simonw/llm (800 forks). Sync reported 'Changed forks: 0' and zero signals after completion.

To fix:
- Wire SyncService → AnalyzerService in sync.py after the fork loop, for repos that have changed forks and a configured Anthropic token
- Or: add a separate 'forkhub analyze' command that runs the analyzer on recently-changed forks
- Also fix: new forks should get compared via provider.compare() to populate commits_ahead/commits_behind on first discovery
title: Sync pipeline never invokes analyzer — no signals generated

description:
The backfill feature is well-plumbed but the core mechanism is broken in production and isn't actually agentic.

DIRECTION: get a completely non-AI (deterministic) backfill working and proven first — apply fork changes + run tests — then decide whether an AI evaluation/repair layer is actually needed. Today the AI layer is half-wired (dead evaluator prompt) and the deterministic core doesn't even apply real patches.

KEY FINDINGS:
1. CRITICAL: Fetched patches can't be applied. GitHubProvider.get_file_diff (github.py:258) returns the GitHub compare 'patch' field — hunks only, no diff --git/---/+++ headers. backfill.py joins these and runs git apply with no header reconstruction, so real candidates land in PATCH_FAILED/CONFLICT. The 100% suite hides this: stubs inject fully-formed diffs (test_backfill.py:989). [FIXED in 0ff5117 / PR #10]
2. The run loop is not agentic — purely mechanical rank→apply→test. BACKFILL_EVALUATOR_PROMPT (prompts.py:110) is dead code, never imported.
3. Wrong diff scope: get_file_diff compares upstream:default...fork:default — the fork's ENTIRE divergence, not the signal's change. Plain git apply, no --3way.
4. Auto-fix-tests rewrites the upstream project's own tests to accommodate fork behavior, then marks ACCEPTED at 0.8 — inverts the meaning of a green suite.
5. Cluster membership claimed in docstrings (backfill.py:37,173) but never used in ranking.
6. Silent partial fetch (backfill.py:298), no clean-tree precondition, transient failures permanently burn a signal.
7. spec.md has no backfill section at all.

(Recreated from an external agent's backlog; original beads were stranded in an unsynced environment.)
notes: Deterministic path landed in PR #14 (feature/backfill-deterministic): icz, 1u6, slh, 2oc, ntr, 95z all closed. Epic stays open pending forkhub-dvi (AI go/no-go — needs measured hit-rate from live runs per specs/backfill-ai-decision.md).
title: Epic: Rework agentic backfill — prove a deterministic cherry-pick path before keeping the AI layer

description:
## Wave 1 — Foundation (parallelizable with 1.1, 1.2, 1.3)

### File: `src/forkhub/config.py`

### What to build
`ForkHubSettings` using Pydantic Settings with TOML config file + environment variable overrides.

### Settings classes (nested BaseModel groups under one BaseSettings)

```python
class GitHubSettings(BaseModel):
    token: str = ""
    username: str = ""

class AnthropicSettings(BaseModel):
    api_key: str = ""
    analysis_budget_usd: float = 0.50
    model: str = "sonnet"
    digest_model: str = "haiku"

class DatabaseSettings(BaseModel):
    path: str = "~/.local/share/forkhub/forkhub.db"

class SyncSettings(BaseModel):
    polling_interval: str = "6h"
    max_forks_per_repo: int = 5000
    max_github_requests_per_hour: int = 4000

class AnalysisSettings(BaseModel):
    max_deep_dives_per_fork: int = 10

class EmbeddingSettings(BaseModel):
    provider: str = "local"
    model: str = "all-MiniLM-L6-v2"

class DigestSettings(BaseModel):
    frequency: str = "weekly"
    day_of_week: str = "monday"
    time: str = "09:00"
    min_significance: int = 5
    backends: list[str] = ["console"]

class TrackingSettings(BaseModel):
    default_fork_depth: int = 1
    auto_discover_owned: bool = True
    track_sibling_forks: bool = True

class ForkHubSettings(BaseSettings):
    github: GitHubSettings = GitHubSettings()
    anthropic: AnthropicSettings = AnthropicSettings()
    database: DatabaseSettings = DatabaseSettings()
    sync: SyncSettings = SyncSettings()
    analysis: AnalysisSettings = AnalysisSettings()
    embedding: EmbeddingSettings = EmbeddingSettings()
    digest: DigestSettings = DigestSettings()
    tracking: TrackingSettings = TrackingSettings()
```

### Config source priority
Override `settings_customise_sources`: init args > env vars > TOML file > defaults

### TOML file resolution
Search order: `./forkhub.toml`, then `~/.config/forkhub/forkhub.toml`

### Env var format
Prefix: `FORKHUB_`, nested delimiter: `__`
Example: `FORKHUB_GITHUB__TOKEN=ghp_xxx`, `FORKHUB_DIGEST__FREQUENCY=daily`

### Sensitive field handling
Token and api_key fields should use `SecretStr` or be excluded from `__repr__` to prevent accidental logging.

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_config.py`)
- Test defaults load with no config file and no env vars
- Test TOML file loading (create temp toml file)
- Test env var overrides work (monkeypatch env)
- Test nested config via env vars (e.g., `FORKHUB_DIGEST__FREQUENCY=daily`)
- Test config file path resolution (project root found first)
- Test sensitive fields (token, api_key) not in repr/str output
- Test path expansion (`~` expands to home dir in database.path)

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 0 scaffolding (forkhub-2kl)
title: Configuration system (Pydantic Settings + TOML)

description:
## Wave 6 — Public API + Integration

### Files
- `src/forkhub/__init__.py` — ForkHub class (public API)
- `tests/test_integration.py` — end-to-end tests

### What to build: ForkHub Class

The main entry point for library consumers. Composes all services and exposes a clean async API.

```python
class ForkHub:
    def __init__(
        self,
        settings: ForkHubSettings | None = None,
        git_provider: GitProvider | None = None,
        notification_backends: list[NotificationBackend] | None = None,
        embedding_provider: EmbeddingProvider | None = None,
    ):
        # Load settings (or use provided)
        # Create default providers if not injected:
        #   - GitHubProvider(settings.github.token) if git_provider is None
        #   - [ConsoleBackend()] if notification_backends is None
        #   - LocalEmbeddingProvider() if embedding_provider is None
        # Initialize Database from settings.database.path
        # Wire up all services: TrackerService, SyncService, ClusterService, DigestService, AnalyzerService

    async def __aenter__(self): await self._db.init(); return self
    async def __aexit__(self, *args): await self._db.close()

    async def init(self, username: str) -> list[TrackedRepo]:
        """First-time setup: discover owned repos, detect upstreams."""
    async def track(self, owner: str, repo: str, mode: TrackingMode = TrackingMode.watched, depth: int = 1) -> TrackedRepo: ...
    async def untrack(self, owner: str, repo: str) -> None: ...
    async def sync(self, repo: str | None = None, full: bool = False) -> SyncResult:
        """Full sync pipeline: discover forks, compare, analyze, cluster."""
    async def get_repos(self, mode: TrackingMode | None = None) -> list[TrackedRepo]: ...
    async def get_forks(self, owner: str, repo: str, active_only: bool = False) -> list[Fork]: ...
    async def get_clusters(self, owner: str, repo: str, min_size: int = 2) -> list[Cluster]: ...
    async def generate_digest(self, since: datetime | None = None, repo: str | None = None) -> Digest: ...
    async def deliver_digest(self, digest: Digest) -> list[DeliveryResult]: ...
```

### Key design decisions
- Async context manager for DB lifecycle
- All providers are injectable (Protocol-based) for testing and customization
- Default providers created from settings if not provided
- Library consumers can swap any provider (e.g., custom GitProvider for GitLab)

### What to build: Integration Tests

Full pipeline tests exercising real DB + real embeddings + respx-mocked GitHub HTTP:

1. **Full lifecycle test**: init(username) → discovers repos → sync() → signals stored in DB → clusters detected → generate_digest() → digest has content → deliver_digest() → console output
2. **Watched repo test**: track("vercel/next.js") → sync() → forks discovered → signals → digest includes watched section
3. **Upstream tracking test**: init finds user's forks → upstream parents tracked → sync() → upstream releases detected → signal with is_upstream=True
4. **Empty sync test**: sync() when no forks have changed → no signals, no analysis triggered
5. **Custom provider injection test**: pass custom GitProvider and verify it's used instead of default

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_forkhub_api.py`)
- Test ForkHub class construction with defaults
- Test custom provider injection
- Test async context manager (aenter/aexit)
- Test all public methods delegate to correct services

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Waves 4 and 5 complete (all services, agent layer, CLI)
title: ForkHub public API class and integration tests

close_reason: Switched _apply_and_test to git apply --3way with working-tree reset on conflict; made apply_signal diff fetching all-or-nothing (partial fetch -> PATCH_FAILED, empty diff skipped, only patched files staged). Added drift-resolves/conflict and partial-fetch/empty-diff tests. 100% coverage, lint/format clean, types at baseline 18.
description: backfill.py:_apply_and_test uses plain git apply with no fallback, so any context drift between the fork's diff base and the local tree fails hard. Switch to git apply --3way (with index) so near-misses resolve or produce real conflict markers/CONFLICT status. Also: backfill.py:298-299 silently logs and drops a file whose diff failed to fetch, then proceeds with a partial patch set — decide on all-or-nothing semantics and surface partial-fetch as an explicit failure rather than committing a half-applied feature.
title: Apply backfill patches with git apply --3way and handle context drift + partial fetch

close_reason: Already fixed in commit 0ff5117 (PR #10): _reconstruct_diff in GitHubProvider.get_file_diff rebuilds diff --git/---/+++ headers around hunk-only compare payloads, handles added/removed/renamed/copied statuses, returns empty for binary/pure-rename. Verified with real git apply + content assertions. Caveat documented: file mode not preserved (hardcoded 100644).
description:
CRITICAL — the deterministic core does not work on real data.

GitHubProvider.get_file_diff (src/forkhub/providers/github.py:248) returns file_data.get('patch','') from the compare API. That field is hunks only (starts with @@), with NO diff --git a/.. b/.., no --- a/path, no +++ b/path. BackfillService.apply_signal (src/forkhub/services/backfill.py:286-307) collects these, joins with newlines, and runs git apply --check - (backfill.py:402) with no header reconstruction. git apply rejects headerless input, so every real candidate becomes PATCH_FAILED/CONFLICT.

Why tests miss it: stubs inject fully-formed diffs, e.g. tests/test_backfill.py:989 (--- a/src/cache.py / +++ b/src/cache.py / @@ ...). That shape never comes from the real provider.

Fix options: fetch an applyable patch (e.g. GET /compare/{base}...{head}.diff, or the fork commit-range .patch), OR reconstruct diff --git/---/+++ headers around each file's hunks in get_file_diff. Must be verified against a real GitHub payload.
title: Fix backfill patch fetching: diffs lack git headers and cannot be applied

## Wave 0 — Must complete before all other work

### What to build
1. Run `uv init` with src layout in the forkhub project directory
2. Configure `pyproject.toml`:
   - Dependencies: `githubkit`, `claude-agent-sdk`, `typer[all]`, `rich`, `pydantic>=2.0`, `pydantic-settings`, `sentence-transformers`, `sqlite-vec`, `aiosqlite`

   - Script entry point: `forkhub = "forkhub.cli.app:app"`

6. Create minimal `src/forkhub/cli/app.py` with `app = typer.Typer(name="forkhub", help="Monitor GitHub fork constellations")`
7. Create `forkhub.toml.example` from spec §12
8. Init git repo, create comprehensive `.gitignore`
9. Run `uv sync` to install everything
10. All `.py` files must start with 2-line ABOUTME comments

### Smoke tests

close_reason: Add NEEDS_REVIEW BackfillStatus: auto_fix_tests success now marks needs_review (score None, kept branch) instead of auto-accepting at 0.8; CLI exit code 5, EXPERIMENTAL --auto-fix-tests warning, run_backfill counts/branches. 518 tests pass, 100% cov, ty baseline 18.
description:
When a fork patch breaks the suite, _attempt_test_fix (backfill.py:460) asks Haiku to rewrite the UPSTREAM project's OWN tests to accommodate the imported behavior, then sets status ACCEPTED, score 0.8 (backfill.py:448-450). That inverts the meaning of a passing suite — it edits the oracle to match the change. Until/unless the AI layer is justified, gate this hard: never auto-accept a patch whose acceptance required editing the project's tests; mark it for human review instead. Make the behavior and its risk explicit in CLI help.

Note: partially mitigated already — 'Change auto_fix_tests default to False' was closed previously, so the path is opt-in. The hard gate (no auto-ACCEPT after test edits) and CLI help warning are still open.
design: DECIDED by La Boeuf 2026-06-07: add NEEDS_REVIEW BackfillStatus member. When _attempt_test_fix succeeds, status = NEEDS_REVIEW (not ACCEPTED), patch + test edits stay on the candidate branch, never auto-accepted, not counted in result.accepted. CLI help for --auto-fix-tests states the risk explicitly.
title: Guard auto-fix-tests: stop silently marking ACCEPTED (0.8) after rewriting upstream tests

description:
## Wave 5 — CLI Layer

### Files
All in `src/forkhub/cli/`:
- `app.py` — Root Typer app, composes all subcommands
- `init_cmd.py` — `forkhub init`
- `track_cmd.py` — `track`, `untrack`, `exclude`, `include`
- `repos_cmd.py` — `repos`
- `forks_cmd.py` — `forks`, `inspect`
- `clusters_cmd.py` — `clusters`
- `sync_cmd.py` — `sync`
- `digest_cmd.py` — `digest`, `digest-config`
- `config_cmd.py` — `config show/set/path`

### Architecture principle
CLI layer is THIN. Each command:
1. Parses arguments (Typer handles this)
2. Loads config, initializes services
3. Calls library service method
4. Formats output with Rich (using formatting.py helpers)

### Command specifications

**init** (`init_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub init --user <github_username> --token <github_token>`
- Interactive first-time setup. Stores token in config file, discovers owned repos, detects upstream repos
- Shows Rich table of discovered repos with [owned]/[upstream] labels
- Creates config directory if needed

**track/untrack/exclude/include** (`track_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub track <owner/repo> --depth <n>` — calls TrackerService.track_repo, shows confirmation
- `forkhub untrack <owner/repo>` — calls TrackerService.untrack_repo, shows confirmation
- `forkhub exclude <repo_name>` — calls TrackerService.exclude_repo
- `forkhub include <repo_name>` — calls TrackerService.include_repo

**repos** (`repos_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub repos [--owned|--watched|--upstream]` — Rich table: name, mode, forks count, last synced, excluded status

**forks** (`forks_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub forks <owner/repo> --active --sort [significance|stars|recent|ahead] --category <cat> --limit <n>`
- Rich table: fork name, vitality, stars, commits ahead, top signal summary, significance
- `forkhub inspect <fork_full_name>` — detailed view: all signals for this fork, star history, files changed

**clusters** (`clusters_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub clusters <owner/repo> --min-size <n>` — Rich panels showing each cluster: label, description, fork count, member signals

**sync** (`sync_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub sync [--repo <owner/repo>] [--full]`
- Shows Rich progress bar during sync
- After sync: summary of forks discovered, forks changed, signals created, releases found
- `--full` forces re-crawl ignoring HEAD SHA cache

**digest** (`digest_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub digest [--since <date>] [--dry-run] [--repo <owner/repo>]`
- `--dry-run` generates digest and prints to console without marking as delivered
- `forkhub digest-config --frequency [daily|weekly|on_demand] --day [mon-sun] --time HH:MM --min-significance <n> --categories <list> --files <patterns> --backends <list>`

**config** (`config_cmd.py`):
- `forkhub config show` — pretty-print current config (mask sensitive values)
- `forkhub config set <key> <value>` — set a config value in TOML file
- `forkhub config path` — show config file location

### Common pattern for all commands
```python
import asyncio
from forkhub.config import ForkHubSettings
from forkhub.database import Database

def _get_services():
    """Initialize config, DB, and services. Used by all commands."""
    settings = ForkHubSettings()
    db = Database(settings.database.path)
    # ... return services tuple
```

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_cli.py` or split per command)
Use Typer's `CliRunner`:
- Test each command with valid inputs produces expected output
- Test error cases (repo not found, not initialized, invalid args)
- Test `--help` for every command
- Test init creates config file
- Test repos/forks/clusters display correct table output
- For sync/digest: test that they invoke the correct service methods

### Dependencies
- Non-agent commands (repos, forks, track, config, clusters) can start once Wave 3 services exist
- Agent-dependent commands (sync with analysis, digest) wait for Wave 4
- All commands need Wave 1 (models, config, database)
title: CLI commands: init, track, repos, forks, clusters, sync, digest, config

description: UAT Tier 3: the Agent SDK launches the bundled Claude Code CLI as a subprocess; inside an existing Claude Code session the CLAUDECODE env var makes it refuse to start ('cannot be launched inside another Claude Code session'), so sync completes with 0 signals. Compounding: the fatal session error is swallowed by runner.py's logger.exception with no logging configured (forkhub-uox corroboration #3) — the user just sees 'New signals: 0'. Actions: (1) README/spec note for developers running forkhub from within Claude Code (env -u CLAUDECODE), (2) consider surfacing agent-session failures in the sync CLI summary as warnings (like compare errors already are) instead of log-only.
title: Document: forkhub sync's agent analysis cannot run inside a Claude Code session (CLAUDECODE guard)

close_reason: Shipped in PR #6 (feature/backfill-decoupling): TestFixer protocol extracted, claude-agent-sdk moved to [claude] optional extra, CLI primitives sub-app with 10 subcommands for external-agent-driven backfill
description:
Expose composable backfill primitives via CLI so external agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Aider, local models, humans, shell scripts) can drive the test-fix loop without ForkHub depending on any specific model provider.

New CLI subcommands:
- forkhub backfill candidates [--repo X] [--json]: List signals eligible for backfill
- forkhub backfill apply <signal-id>: Apply a single patch, run tests, exit 0 on pass / 1 on fail
- forkhub backfill read-failures [--json]: Print failing test file paths and contents
- forkhub backfill write-test <path>: Write a test file from stdin (with safety gate from _is_test_file)
- forkhub backfill record <signal-id> --status=accepted|rejected: Record the attempt outcome
- forkhub backfill cleanup <signal-id>: Return to original branch, delete candidate branch

All commands output JSON when --json is passed. This lets:
- Claude Code agents drive the loop via bash tool calls
- Aider/Cursor drive it via their shell execution
- Local Ollama or any agent framework compose primitives
- Shell scripts and humans do it manually

Keeps the unix composition philosophy: ForkHub provides primitives, agents compose them.
title: Add backfill CLI primitives for external agents

description:
## Wave 3 — Services (depends on Tracker service)

### File: `src/forkhub/services/sync.py`

### What to build
`SyncService` orchestrates the sync pipeline: discover forks, compare HEAD SHAs, detect changes, check releases, update vitality/stars.

### Class: `SyncService(db: Database, provider: GitProvider, tracker: TrackerService, settings: SyncSettings)`

### Data types
```python
@dataclass
class RepoSyncResult:
    repo: TrackedRepo
    new_forks: int
    changed_forks: list[Fork]
    new_releases: list[Release]
    errors: list[str]

@dataclass
class SyncResult:
    repos_synced: int
    total_changed_forks: int
    total_new_releases: int
    results: list[RepoSyncResult]
    errors: list[str]
```

### Methods
- `sync_all() -> SyncResult` — syncs all non-excluded tracked repos. Uses `asyncio.gather()` with semaphore for concurrency.
- `sync_repo(repo: TrackedRepo) -> RepoSyncResult`:
  1. Discover forks via `provider.get_forks()` (paginated), save/update in DB
  2. For each fork: compare current HEAD SHA with stored `fork.head_sha` — skip if unchanged
  3. For changed forks: fetch `provider.compare()` data, update fork record (commits_ahead, commits_behind, head_sha)
  4. Check for new releases via `provider.get_releases(since=repo.last_synced_at)`
  5. Update fork vitality based on `last_pushed_at`
  6. Update star counts and calculate velocity (stars - stars_previous)
  7. Update `repo.last_synced_at`
  8. Return RepoSyncResult with changed forks and new releases
- `_classify_vitality(last_pushed_at: datetime | None) -> ForkVitality` — active if pushed within 90 days, dormant if 90-365, dead if >365, unknown if no push date

### Error handling
- If a single fork comparison fails (404 — fork deleted, etc.), log error and continue with remaining forks
- If rate limit is hit, stop sync early and report partial results

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_sync.py`)
Use real in-memory SQLite + stub GitProvider:
- Test full sync pipeline: discovers forks, compares, returns changed list
- Test HEAD SHA comparison: unchanged forks are skipped
- Test changed forks get updated head_sha, commits_ahead, commits_behind
- Test vitality classification (active/dormant/dead boundaries)
- Test star velocity calculation
- Test new release detection with since filtering
- Test error handling: individual fork failure doesn't stop sync
- Test sync_all aggregates results from multiple repos
- Test last_synced_at gets updated

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Tracker service, Wave 2 (providers)
title: Sync service (fork discovery, comparison, change detection)

description:
## Wave 1 — Foundation (parallelizable with 1.1, 1.2, 1.4)

### File: `src/forkhub/database.py`

### What to build
`Database` class using `aiosqlite` for async SQLite operations with sqlite-vec extension for vector similarity search.

### Database class
- `Database(path: str | Path)` — async context manager
- `async init()` — creates DB file, loads sqlite-vec extension, runs schema DDL
- Uses `uuid4()` strings for all primary keys

### Schema (from spec §8)
All tables from the spec: `tracked_repos`, `forks`, `signals`, `clusters`, `cluster_members`, `digest_configs`, `digests`, `annotations`, `sync_state`. Plus all indexes.

Vector table:
```sql
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE vec_signals USING vec0(
    signal_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    embedding float[384]
);
```

### CRUD methods
Organized by entity:
- `save_tracked_repo()`, `get_tracked_repo(id)`, `get_tracked_repo_by_name(full_name)`, `list_tracked_repos(mode=None)`, `delete_tracked_repo(id)`, `update_tracked_repo()`
- `save_fork()`, `get_fork(id)`, `get_fork_by_name(full_name)`, `list_forks_for_repo(repo_id)`, `update_fork()`

- `save_cluster()`, `get_clusters_for_repo(repo_id, min_size=2)`, `add_cluster_member(cluster_id, signal_id, fork_id)`, `update_cluster()`
- `save_digest_config()`, `get_digest_config(repo_id=None)`, `get_global_digest_config()`
- `save_digest()`, `get_latest_digest(config_id)`
- `set_sync_state(key, value)`, `get_sync_state(key) -> str | None`

### Vector operations
- `save_signal_embedding(signal_id: str, embedding: list[float])` — uses `sqlite_vec.serialize_float32()`
- `search_similar_signals(embedding: list[float], limit: int = 5) -> list[tuple[str, float]]` — returns (signal_id, distance) pairs

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_database.py`)

description:
## Wave 2 — Providers (parallelizable with 2.2, 2.3)

### File: `src/forkhub/providers/github.py`

### What to build
`GitHubProvider` class implementing the `GitProvider` Protocol using the `githubkit` library.

### Class design
- Constructor: `GitHubProvider(token: str)` — creates `GitHub(token, auto_retry=RetryRateLimit(max_retry=2))` client
- `asyncio.Semaphore(10)` for concurrent GitHub API call control
- All methods are async, return our Pydantic models (not githubkit models)

### Method implementations
1. `get_user_repos(username)` — `github.rest.repos.async_list_for_authenticated_user()` with pagination via `github.rest.paginate()`, map to `list[RepoInfo]`
2. `get_forks(owner, repo, page)` — `github.rest.repos.async_list_forks()` with pagination, map to `ForkPage`. Calculate `has_diverged` by checking if fork's pushed_at > created_at.
3. `compare(owner, repo, base, head)` — `github.rest.repos.async_compare_commits(owner, repo, basehead=f"{base}...{head}")`, map to `CompareResult` with files and commits
4. `get_releases(owner, repo, since)` — `github.rest.repos.async_list_releases()`, filter by `published_at > since`, map to `list[Release]`
5. `get_repo(owner, repo)` — `github.rest.repos.async_get()`, map to `RepoInfo`
6. `get_commit_messages(owner, repo, since)` — `github.rest.repos.async_list_commits(since=since)`, map to `list[CommitInfo]`
7. `get_file_diff(owner, repo, base, head, path)` — compare then extract the specific file's patch string from the files list
8. `get_rate_limit()` — `github.rest.rate_limit.async_get()`, map to `RateLimitInfo`

### Mapping helpers
Private `_to_repo_info()`, `_to_fork_info()`, etc. methods to convert githubkit response models to our Pydantic models.

### Error handling
- `PrimaryRateLimitExceeded` — re-raise as custom `RateLimitError`
- `RequestFailed` with 404 — re-raise as custom `RepoNotFoundError`
- `RequestFailed` with other codes — re-raise as custom `GitProviderError`
- Define these exceptions in `src/forkhub/exceptions.py`

### ETag caching
githubkit has built-in HTTP cache support — leverage it. Unchanged resources return 304 and use cached data automatically.

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_github_provider.py`)
Use `respx` (httpx mock library) since githubkit uses httpx internally:
- Test each of the 8 methods maps API responses to correct Pydantic models
- Test pagination for repos and forks (multi-page responses)
- Test rate limit info extraction
- Test error handling: 404 raises RepoNotFoundError, 403 rate limit raises RateLimitError
- Test semaphore limits concurrency
- Fixture files: `tests/fixtures/github/repos.json`, `forks.json`, `compare.json`, `releases.json`, `rate_limit.json`

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 1 (models, interfaces, config)

description:
Make forkhub's service layer optionally emit structured events as JSONL files so brooklet (or any JSONL consumer: tail -f, jq, custom tools) can observe forkhub operations as a stream rather than reconstructing state from DB queries or log lines.

## Motivation

Forkhub currently has no event stream. Observability comes from:
- DB queries (state, not change events)
- Python logging (human-readable, unstructured, not consumption-oriented)
- --json CLI output (one-shot per command invocation, not a stream)

Meanwhile, brooklet (~/Dropbox/python_workspace/brooklet) is a JSONL-based event streaming library with gapless resume, offset tracking per consumer group, follow mode, and derived-topic support via a scout-style plugin pattern. Forkhub's service layer creates a bunch of discrete, timestamped events that map cleanly onto brooklet topics — making forkhub into a brooklet producer is a really natural pairing.

Zero forkhub dependency on brooklet: forkhub writes plain JSONL to files; brooklet reads them as topics. That's the 'echo >> is the universal producer API' philosophy brooklet is built around.

## Proposed topics and events

| Topic | Event | Emitted by | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| forkhub/forks | fork.discovered, fork.status-changed, fork.commits-updated | SyncService | Phase 1 |
| forkhub/backfill-attempts | backfill.attempt.applied, backfill.attempt.recorded, backfill.attempt.cleaned | BackfillService | Phase 1 |
| forkhub/digests | digest.generated, digest.delivered | DigestService | Phase 1 |
| forkhub/clusters | cluster.formed, cluster.grew, cluster.dissolved | ClusterService | Phase 1 |
| forkhub/signals | signal.created | AnalyzerService | Phase 2 (depends on forkhub-hgm) |

Phase 1 is shippable today and has immediate value for the services that already exist. Phase 2 unblocks once the analyzer decoupling epic (forkhub-7qk) lands and sync actually produces signals.

## Event shape

Brooklet's envelope is thin (_ts, _seq, _src auto-injected), so forkhub just writes its own payload:

{"event":"signal.created","signal_id":"abc123","fork":"sp6370/claude-plugins","tracked_repo":"JoshuaOliphant/claude-plugins","category":"feature","summary":"Adds retry middleware","significance":7,"files_involved":["src/middleware.py"]}

## Implementation sketch

1. **New config setting**: event_stream_dir: Path | None in ForkHubSettings. When None (default), no events emitted — zero overhead, full backward compat. FORKHUB_EVENTS_DIR env var and [events] section in forkhub.toml.

2. **New module src/forkhub/events.py** with a tiny EventEmitter:

    class EventEmitter:
        def __init__(self, stream_dir: Path | None) -> None:
            self._dir = stream_dir

        def emit(self, topic: str, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
            if self._dir is None:
                return
            path = self._dir / f"{topic}.jsonl"
            path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
            with path.open("a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
                f.write(json.dumps(event, default=str) + "\n")

   No brooklet dependency — forkhub writes plain JSONL, brooklet reads them as files.

3. **Inject into services**: SyncService, BackfillService, DigestService, ClusterService accept an optional events: EventEmitter | None = None parameter. Each service emits at natural boundaries. AnalyzerService gets the same treatment in Phase 2.

4. **ForkHub facade** constructs the EventEmitter from settings and passes it to each service.

5. **Tests**: StubEventEmitter that appends to an in-memory list; assert events are emitted at the right boundaries with the right payloads. Each service's existing test suite gets a class like TestEventEmission covering the new boundaries.

6. **README**: document the [events] config section with a brooklet integration example.

## Integration example (target state)

    # One-time setup
    export FORKHUB_EVENTS_DIR=~/.local/share/forkhub/events
    export BROOKLET_DIR=~/.local/share/brooklet
    brooklet register forkhub-all "~/.local/share/forkhub/events/*.jsonl" --mode glob

    # Live dashboard of new signals/backfills
    brooklet watch forkhub-all --group dashboard

    # Gapless resume across sessions, filter for high-significance only
    brooklet consume forkhub-all --group my-monitor | jq 'select(.significance >= 8)'

A forkhub scout-style brooklet plugin could later consume the raw topics and produce derived ones (forkhub/weekly-signal-stats, forkhub/fork-vitality-changes, etc).

## Phasing

- **Phase 1** (ship now): fork, backfill, digest, cluster events. All services exist today, immediate value.
- **Phase 2** (ship after forkhub-hgm): add signal events once the analyzer wiring lands.

Split into two beads later if preferred, but the EventEmitter module is the same work and phase 2 is ~5 lines of glue.

## Related

- Downstream consumer target: brooklet (~/Dropbox/python_workspace/brooklet)
- Phase 2 depends on: forkhub-hgm (analyzer wiring into sync)
title: feat: optional event stream emission as JSONL topics

description:
Complete the library-first, BYO-LLM architecture by applying the same decoupling pattern to the analysis pipeline that we did for backfill in v0.3.0.

Context: v0.3.0 extracted a TestFixer Protocol, moved claude-agent-sdk to an optional [claude] extra, and added CLI primitives that let any external agent (Claude Code, Cursor, Aider, local models, shell scripts) drive the backfill test-fix loop. The analysis pipeline is the last piece of ForkHub that's still hardcoded to Claude — AnalysisRunner uses a full ClaudeSDKClient session with MCP tools, subagents, hooks, and budgets baked into src/forkhub/agent/runner.py.

Architecture target:
- src/forkhub/interfaces.py gets an Analyzer Protocol
- src/forkhub/agent/runner.py's AnalysisRunner → ClaudeAnalyzer (one concrete impl)
- SyncService depends on the Protocol, not the Claude-specific runner
- CLI primitives let external agents drive analysis without the [claude] extra

Key difference from the TestFixer refactor: analysis is open-ended exploration (agent decides which of 30 forks to investigate, which files to diff). The one-shot query pattern that worked for test fixing won't work here. External agents need composable CLI primitives they can call from shell, not a one-shot 'classify this JSON' interface.

Also fixes forkhub-0tf (the sync pipeline never invokes analyzer) as a side effect of step 2.

Sub-issues (three-step path):
1. Extract Analyzer Protocol (mirror TestFixer)
2. Wire SyncService → Analyzer (fixes forkhub-0tf)
3. Add analysis CLI primitives for external agents

Wrinkles to watch for:
- Clustering/dedup loop: the current analyzer uses search_similar_signals via MCP to avoid duplicates. External agents would need to call 'signal search' explicitly for every signal they want to store.
- Batching: the 30-forks-per-session batch size is Claude Sonnet specific. Different models want different batch sizes; the primitive case has no batching concept.
- Cost observability: current runner has a cost tracker hook aggregating spend in sync_state. CLI primitives lose that unless we add a 'spend log' command.
- Analysis is harder to drive manually than test fixing — 'here are 800 forks, find the 20 interesting ones' is a judgment call the driving agent has to make.
title: Epic: Decouple Analyzer from Claude (mirror TestFixer refactor)

description:
The v0.4.0 sync wiring (forkhub-618 + forkhub-hgm) added a pragmatic fix: new active forks get compared on first discovery, so their commits_ahead/commits_behind are populated. But the deeper bug remains: Fork.head_sha is never populated from the real GitHub API (GitHubProvider.get_forks returns ForkInfo which has no head_sha field — the /forks endpoint doesn't include it). This means on subsequent syncs, old_sha == new_sha == None, so the compare() branch never fires and changes after first discovery go undetected.

Fix options:
1. Add an extra /repos/{owner}/{repo}/commits/{branch} call per fork to fetch HEAD SHA. Costs 1 API call per fork per sync — expensive for repos with hundreds of forks.
2. Use the git refs API once to fetch all fork HEADs in a single call (if available).
3. Track a 'last_analyzed_at' timestamp per fork and fall back to last_pushed_at comparison when head_sha is None.

Discovered while implementing forkhub-hgm / smoke-testing v0.3.0 against simonw/llm (800 forks). Not a regression — this has been the behavior since the sync pipeline landed.
title: Populate Fork.head_sha from GitHub API for reliable change detection

close_reason: Dropped the ACTIVE-vitality gate: new forks (any vitality) are compared + baselined on first discovery; existing forks compare on pushed_at advance OR NULL head_sha (one-time catch-up). Budget preserved: unchanged baselined forks cost zero extra calls (asserted via stub call recording). Bullet dormant regression covered.
description: UAT Tier 1 vs live GitHub (bchao1/bullet): all 115 forks pushed >365d ago → vitality DEAD → first-discovery compare skipped (sync.py:246-252 gates on ACTIVE); existing-fork compare only runs when pushed_at advances (sync.py:290) — quiet forks never advance → compared NEVER. Result on the product's canonical scenario (dormant upstream, diverged-but-quiet forks): ahead/behind stay 0 forever, changed_forks always 0, analyzer never invoked, zero signals, zero backfill candidates — chroma-core/bullet's 2-commit Windows-support divergence is invisible end-to-end. Needs a design decision on API budget: e.g. compare ALL forks once at first discovery (115 compare calls is well within 4000/hr), or a one-time baseline pass, or sampling. Blocks meaningful Tier 3 UAT.
title: Vitality gate starves compare: dormant-upstream constellations never get ahead/behind, signals, or candidates

description: UAT: running forkhub from /tmp/forkhub-uat (or any dir without .env) silently yields empty GITHUB_TOKEN/CLAUDE creds because load_dotenv_file() (config.py) defaults to cwd-only .env discovery. Combined with the empty-token-header bug this produced a whole UAT run on unauthenticated access without any warning. Options: search upward for .env (like git/ruff do), honor a documented global location (~/.config/forkhub/.env), or log which credential sources were found at startup. Lower priority than the header bug but they compound.
title: load_dotenv_file only reads .env from cwd — forkhub run from any other dir gets empty token silently

description: Requested by project owner. Each fork analysis sends a large stable preamble — COORDINATOR_PROMPT, the 7 MCP tool definitions, and the diff-analyst subagent prompt — that is identical across forks within a sync and across syncs. At observed ~$0.56/fork (Tier 3, single fork, inflated by the store_signal retry bug but still), a 100-fork constellation is expensive; prompt caching on the stable prefix (cache_control breakpoints) can cut input cost 70-90% on the cached portion. Investigate: (1) does claude-agent-sdk enable prompt caching by default for system_prompt + tools, or expose a knob in ClaudeAgentOptions? (2) if not automatic, set cache_control on the coordinator system prompt and tool definitions; (3) verify cache hits via ResultMessage usage (cache_read_input_tokens) in an instrumented run — ties into the observability harness. Reference ~/.claude docs / claude-api skill for current caching params. Note batching: ClaudeAnalyzer batches 30 forks/session, so the cached prefix is reused across the batch — confirm the SDK keeps the cache warm within a session.
title: Ensure the analyzer leverages Anthropic prompt caching for the stable system prompt + tool defs

description: UAT Tier 2 probe (live API): a signal whose fork repo 404s (deleted/renamed fork — common in real constellations) crashes 'backfill apply' with a full GitHubProviderError traceback and exit 1 — colliding with exit 1 = tests_failed in the documented contract, and no attempt row is recorded (the trace is lost, though the signal isn't burned). Root cause: apply_signal's fetch loop catches (OSError, ConnectionError, TimeoutError, RuntimeError) but GitHubProviderError (providers/github.py:23) inherits Exception directly. The 1u6 partial-fetch tests pass because stubs raise types inside the tuple — the real provider's primary failure mode is the one type not caught. Fix options: add GitHubProviderError to the tuple (couples service to provider module), OR have the GitProvider protocol define a provider-agnostic error type the service catches, OR make GitHubProviderError inherit RuntimeError. Then the 404 path lands in 'Partial fetch' → PATCH_FAILED → exit 3 (retriable is debatable for a 404 — consider a terminal variant). Add a respx test with a 404 compare response driving apply_signal end-to-end.
title: Backfill fetch loop doesn't catch GitHubProviderError — deleted-fork 404 crashes apply with unhandled traceback

description:
## Wave 2 — Providers (parallelizable with 2.1, 2.3)

### File: `src/forkhub/embeddings/local.py`

### What to build
`LocalEmbeddingProvider` implementing the `EmbeddingProvider` Protocol using sentence-transformers with the `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` model.

### Class design
```python
class LocalEmbeddingProvider:
    def __init__(self, model_name: str = "all-MiniLM-L6-v2"):
        self._model_name = model_name
        self._model: SentenceTransformer | None = None  # Lazy loaded

    async def embed(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
        # Lazy load model on first call
        if self._model is None:
            self._model = await asyncio.to_thread(SentenceTransformer, self._model_name)
        # sentence-transformers is sync, wrap in to_thread
        embeddings = await asyncio.to_thread(self._model.encode, texts)
        return embeddings.tolist()

    def dimensions(self) -> int:
        return 384  # all-MiniLM-L6-v2 output dimensions
```

### Key design decisions
- **Lazy loading**: Model is NOT loaded at construction time. First `embed()` call triggers download/load (~22MB model, ~80MB with deps). This keeps import time fast.
- **Thread wrapping**: `model.encode()` is synchronous and CPU-bound. `asyncio.to_thread()` prevents blocking the event loop.
- **Return type**: Convert numpy array to `list[list[float]]` via `.tolist()` for clean Pydantic/JSON serialization.

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_embeddings.py`)
These are real integration tests — they load the actual model (per project rules: no mocks):
- Test `embed()` returns correct dimensions (384 per text)
- Test batch embedding of multiple texts returns correct count
- Test single text embedding
- Test empty list input returns empty list
- Test lazy loading: model is None after construction, loaded after first embed()
- Test `dimensions()` returns 384
- Test embeddings are normalized (cosine similarity friendly)
- Test similar texts produce closer embeddings than dissimilar texts

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 1 (interfaces)
title: Local embedding provider (sentence-transformers)

description: After the deterministic cherry-pick path is correct and proven (real patches apply, tests gate acceptance, results are useful), decide whether an AI layer adds enough value to justify it. Candidate roles if yes: (a) value/quality/security scoring of a candidate before applying (the original BACKFILL_EVALUATOR_PROMPT intent), (b) repairing the fork's PRODUCTION code conflicts (not rewriting upstream tests). Capture the measured hit-rate of the pure-deterministic path as the baseline for this decision. Blocked until the deterministic path lands.
design:
RECOMMENDATION (2026-06-08, post-UAT): NO-GO on a bespoke AI evaluation/repair layer for backfill. Rationale: (1) deterministic path proven on live data — chroma-core Windows patch cherry-picked ACCEPTED via 3way+test-gate; (2) AI belongs in sync/classification (judgment), not backfill (mechanical git apply — AI doesn't make git apply better); (3) the original BACKFILL_EVALUATOR_PROMPT was dead code nobody missed; (4) the only defensible AI touch (test-fixer) is already shipped, capped at NEEDS_REVIEW (suggest, human decides); expanding AI to resolve production-code conflicts risks semantic errors gated only by possibly-incomplete tests; (5) economics — adds per-candidate LLM cost to a currently-free deterministic path.

Cost levers (cheaper models, prompt caching per forkhub-a8p) apply to the sync classifier we KEEP, not to a backfill layer we shouldn't build — La Boeuf's point: if it's not needed, cost optimization is moot.

REVERSAL CONDITION (the only thing that changes this): run a real 'backfill run' across a larger constellation and measure the deterministic hit-rate. IF a large fraction land CONFLICT (not ACCEPTED/PATCH_FAILED) AND a sample shows those are mechanical drift not genuine divergence, THEN prototype an AI conflict-resolver for the fork's CODE (never tests). Until that number exists, building it is speculation. Measurement is now cheap (harness + fixed pipeline = one command). Stays OPEN pending that hit-rate; not blocking.
title: DECISION: go/no-go on an AI evaluation/repair layer for backfill

## Wave 4 — Agent Layer (parallelizable with 4.1, but runner depends on tools)

### Files
- `src/forkhub/agent/agents.py` — subagent definitions
- `src/forkhub/agent/hooks.py` — Agent SDK hooks
- `src/forkhub/agent/runner.py` — analysis orchestration
- `src/forkhub/services/analyzer.py` — thin library API wrapper

### What to build: Subagent Definitions (`agents.py`)

```python
diff_analyst = AgentDefinition(
    description="Analyzes a single fork in depth to classify its changes",
    prompt=DIFF_ANALYST_PROMPT,  # from prompts.py
    tools=["mcp__forkhub__get_fork_summary", "mcp__forkhub__get_file_diff",
           "mcp__forkhub__get_releases", "mcp__forkhub__get_fork_stars",
           "mcp__forkhub__store_signal", "mcp__forkhub__search_similar_signals"],
    model="sonnet",
)

digest_writer = AgentDefinition(
    description="Composes notification digests from accumulated signals",
    prompt=DIGEST_WRITER_PROMPT,
    model="haiku",
)
```

### What to build: Hooks (`hooks.py`)

1. `cost_tracker` — PostToolUse hook. Increments API call counter in sync_state for any tool starting with `mcp__forkhub__`. Tracks total calls per tool.
2. `rate_limit_guard` — PreToolUse hook. Before GitHub API tool calls (list_forks, get_fork_summary, get_file_diff, get_releases, get_fork_stars), checks rate limit. If remaining < 100, returns deny with message telling agent to focus on already-fetched data.
3. `pre_compact` — PreCompact hook. Logs the compaction event. Analysis progress is safe in DB (stored via store_signal tool calls).

### What to build: Analysis Runner (`runner.py`)

`AnalysisRunner(db: Database, provider: GitProvider, embedding_provider: EmbeddingProvider, settings: ForkHubSettings)`

Main method: `analyze_repo(repo: TrackedRepo, changed_forks: list[Fork], new_releases: list[Release]) -> list[Signal]`:
1. Create MCP server from tools via `create_sdk_mcp_server(name="forkhub", tools=[...])`
2. Configure `ClaudeAgentOptions`:
   - `mcp_servers={"forkhub": mcp_server}`
   - `agents={"diff-analyst": diff_analyst, "digest-writer": digest_writer}`
   - `hooks` with cost_tracker, rate_limit_guard, pre_compact
   - `max_budget_usd` from settings.anthropic.analysis_budget_usd
   - `model` from settings.anthropic.model
3. Build coordinator prompt with: repo context, list of changed forks (names, commits ahead, files changed count), new releases summary
4. Start `ClaudeSDKClient` session, run `client.query()` with coordinator prompt
5. After session completes, query DB for all signals created during this analysis (by created_at > session_start)
6. Return the signals list

**Batching for large constellations:**
- If len(changed_forks) > 30, split into batches of 30
- Run separate agent sessions per batch
- Aggregate signals across batches

### Analyzer wrapper (`services/analyzer.py`)
Thin wrapper that the library API (`ForkHub` class) calls:
```python
class AnalyzerService:
    def __init__(self, runner: AnalysisRunner):
        self._runner = runner

    async def analyze(self, repo, changed_forks, new_releases) -> list[Signal]:
        return await self._runner.analyze_repo(repo, changed_forks, new_releases)
```

### TDD Tests
- `tests/test_agent_hooks.py`:
  - Test cost_tracker increments counter in sync_state
  - Test rate_limit_guard blocks when remaining < 100
  - Test rate_limit_guard allows when remaining >= 100
- `tests/test_agent_runner.py`:
  - Test coordinator prompt construction includes repo context and fork summary
  - Test MCP server creation registers all 7 tools
  - Test options configuration has correct budget, model, hooks
  - Test batching: 60 forks split into 2 batches of 30
  - Integration test (mark @pytest.mark.integration, needs ANTHROPIC_API_KEY): full analysis run

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Agent tools (4.1), Wave 3 services
title: Agent definitions, hooks, and analysis runner

description: THE Tier 3 blocker, root-caused via instrumented agent probe. agent/tools.py store_signal declares files_involved as python 'list' in the @tool simple-dict schema. The claude-agent-sdk simple-schema mapper (claude_agent_sdk/__init__.py:255-267) has NO branch for list/dict — it falls through to else: {'type':'string'}. So the model receives files_involved as a STRING-typed param while the handler/Signal model expects a list; EVERY store_signal call fails server-side validation. Live agent burned 17 turns / $0.56 retrying (incl. delegating to the diff-analyst subagent) before giving up. Net: the core product feature (sync -> classify -> store signal) produces ZERO signals with the real agent. 100% unit coverage missed it because tests call store_signal(args) directly with a python list, never exercising the SDK schema rendering. Fix: pass an explicit JSON-schema dict for store_signal so files_involved is {'type':'array','items':{'type':'string'}} (the SDK passes through dict schemas verbatim per __init__.py:247-254), and have the handler coerce a JSON-string fallback defensively. AUDIT the other 6 tools in create_tools for any non-scalar param with the same defect. Add a test that asserts the RENDERED schema (via create_sdk_mcp_server) types files_involved as array, not just that the handler accepts a list.

description:
The sync pipeline currently discovers forks and classifies vitality but never invokes the analyzer. AnalyzerService exists in src/forkhub/services/analyzer.py as dead code — nothing imports or calls it. Result: the signals table stays empty regardless of how many forks are discovered, and the backfill feature has nothing to operate on.

This is blocking end-to-end testing of backfill in production (as of v0.3.0 smoke testing).

Fix (depends on forkhub-{extract-analyzer-protocol}):

1. SyncService constructor accepts an optional analyzer: Analyzer | None parameter
2. After the fork loop in sync._sync_repo(), if analyzer is not None and result.changed_forks or result.new_releases are non-empty, invoke analyzer.analyze(repo, changed_forks, new_releases) and store the returned signals
3. CLI sync_cmd wires up ClaudeAnalyzer when auto_analyze=True (default True when [claude] extra is installed, False otherwise) — same pattern we used for ClaudeTestFixer in backfill_cmd
4. Add --no-analyze flag to 'forkhub sync' for users who want discovery without classification
5. Also fix the pre-existing 'new forks never get compared' bug (sync.py:225-240): when a fork is new, still call provider.compare() to populate commits_ahead/commits_behind on first discovery

Verification:
- uv run forkhub sync --repo simonw/llm should produce real signals in the signals table
- uv run forkhub backfill candidates should then return the generated signals
- Test suite: add TestSyncAnalyzerIntegration class covering the wiring with StubAnalyzer returning canned signals
- Test suite: add TestSyncNewForkCompare for the compare-on-first-discovery fix

This is step 2 of the three-step analyzer decoupling epic. Closes forkhub-0tf.
title: Wire SyncService → Analyzer to generate signals (fix forkhub-0tf)

## Wave 1 — Foundation (parallelizable with 1.1, 1.3, 1.4)

### File: `src/forkhub/interfaces.py`

### What to build
Define the three `@runtime_checkable` Protocol classes that form the plugin extension points. Uses `typing.Protocol` for structural typing — no registration, no plugin registry.

### Protocols

**GitProvider** (8 async methods):
```python
@runtime_checkable
class GitProvider(Protocol):
    async def get_user_repos(self, username: str) -> list[RepoInfo]: ...
    async def get_forks(self, owner: str, repo: str, *, page: int = 1) -> ForkPage: ...
    async def compare(self, owner: str, repo: str, base: str, head: str) -> CompareResult: ...
    async def get_releases(self, owner: str, repo: str, *, since: datetime | None = None) -> list[Release]: ...
    async def get_repo(self, owner: str, repo: str) -> RepoInfo: ...
    async def get_commit_messages(self, owner: str, repo: str, *, since: str | None = None) -> list[CommitInfo]: ...
    async def get_file_diff(self, owner: str, repo: str, base: str, head: str, path: str) -> str: ...
    async def get_rate_limit(self) -> RateLimitInfo: ...
```

**NotificationBackend**:
```python
@runtime_checkable
class NotificationBackend(Protocol):
    async def deliver(self, digest: Digest) -> DeliveryResult: ...
    def backend_name(self) -> str: ...
```

**EmbeddingProvider**:
```python
@runtime_checkable
class EmbeddingProvider(Protocol):
    async def embed(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]: ...
    def dimensions(self) -> int: ...
```

Import all referenced types from `models.py`.

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_interfaces.py`)
- Create minimal conforming stub classes (just enough to satisfy the Protocol)
- Verify `isinstance(stub, GitProvider)` returns True
- Verify `isinstance(stub, NotificationBackend)` returns True
- Verify `isinstance(stub, EmbeddingProvider)` returns True
- Create non-conforming classes (missing methods) and verify isinstance returns False
- Verify all method signatures match expected parameter/return types

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 0 scaffolding (forkhub-2kl)
- Needs models from 1.1 but can be built concurrently if types are imported from the same file
title: Protocol interfaces (plugin system)

description: Follow-up to forkhub-zaa. The analyzer-amplification harm is fixed (a persistent SHA-fetch failure no longer re-sends the fork to analyze() every sync — gated on real divergence evidence). But the residual remains: because head_sha stays NULL, needs_baseline stays True, so every sync still runs one compare + one head_sha fetch (2 wasted API calls) forever for such a fork. Bounding this fully needs persistence the current schema lacks: a baseline_attempts counter (or last_baseline_attempt_at) on the fork row, capped so we stop retrying baseline after N failures. Bead-zaa deliberately left this (option 2, schema-free) since the API residual is far cheaper than the analyzer spend. Decide if the counter is worth a migration.
title: NULL-baseline fork with persistent SHA-fetch failure still wastes 2 API calls/sync (compare + head_sha)

close_reason: Empty token now degrades to unauthenticated (GitHub() with no auth strategy); wire-level tests assert no malformed 'token ' header. Fixed on feature/sqw-empty-token-auth (3af9444).
description: UAT Tier 3 root cause. GitHubProvider.__init__ does TokenAuthStrategy(token) unconditionally; with token='' (no GITHUB_TOKEN configured) githubkit emits 'Authorization: token ' which the agent MCP tool path rejects: 'GitHub API error 0: Illegal header value b\'token \''. A user who hasn't set a token should get either clean unauthenticated public access (60 req/hr) or a clear actionable 'set GITHUB_TOKEN' error at startup — not a cryptic illegal-header failure surfaced only inside an agent session. Fix: if token is empty, construct GitHub() with no auth strategy (unauthenticated) OR fail fast at CLI startup with a clear message. Note: plain read paths (get_forks/compare/get_file_diff) happened to work unauthenticated during Tiers 1-2, masking this until the agent tools hit it.
title: Empty GITHUB_TOKEN sends malformed 'token ' auth header instead of degrading to unauthenticated

## Wave 1 — Foundation (parallelizable with 1.2, 1.3, 1.4)

### File: `src/forkhub/models.py`

### What to build
Define all Pydantic data models used as the shared vocabulary across the system. These are pure data transfer objects, NOT ORM models.

### Models to define

**Enums (StrEnum):**
- `SignalCategory`: feature, fix, refactor, config, dependency, removal, adaptation, release
- `ForkVitality`: active, dormant, dead, unknown
- `TrackingMode`: owned, watched, upstream

**Git/GitHub models:**
- `RepoInfo`: github_id, owner, name, full_name, default_branch, description, stars, fork_count, is_fork, parent_full_name
- `ForkInfo`: extends RepoInfo fields + commits_ahead, commits_behind, has_diverged, last_pushed_at, head_sha
- `ForkPage`: forks: list[ForkInfo], total_count, has_next_page, next_page
- `CompareResult`: ahead_by, behind_by, files: list[FileChange], commits: list[CommitInfo]
- `FileChange`: filename, status, additions, deletions, patch (optional)
- `CommitInfo`: sha, message, author, date
- `Release`: tag_name, name, body, published_at, prerelease
- `RateLimitInfo`: limit, remaining, reset_at (datetime)

**Domain models:**
- `TrackedRepo`: id (uuid str), github_id, owner, name, full_name, tracking_mode, default_branch, description, fork_depth, excluded, last_synced_at, created_at
- `Fork`: id (uuid str), tracked_repo_id, github_id, owner, full_name, default_branch, description, vitality, stars, stars_previous, depth, last_pushed_at, commits_ahead, commits_behind, head_sha, created_at, updated_at
- `Signal`: id (uuid str), fork_id (optional—null for upstream signals), tracked_repo_id, category (SignalCategory), summary, detail, files_involved (list[str]), significance (1-10), is_upstream, release_tag, created_at
- `Cluster`: id (uuid str), tracked_repo_id, label, description, files_pattern (list[str]), fork_count, signal_ids (list[str]), created_at, updated_at
- `Digest`: id (uuid str), title, body, signal_ids (list[str]), config_id, delivered_at, created_at
- `DigestConfig`: id (uuid str), tracked_repo_id (optional), frequency, day_of_week, time_of_day, min_significance, categories (list[SignalCategory] | None), file_patterns (list[str] | None), backends (list[str]), created_at
- `DeliveryResult`: success (bool), backend_name (str), error (str | None)

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_models.py`)
- Valid construction for each model
- Validation rejects bad data: significance outside 1-10, invalid category enum, etc.
- Serialization roundtrip (model_dump/model_validate)
- JSON array fields serialize correctly (files_involved, signal_ids)
- Enum membership for all three enums
- Optional fields handle None correctly

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 0 scaffolding (forkhub-2kl)
title: Pydantic data models

## Wave 3 — Services (parallelizable with 3.3, 3.4)

### File: `src/forkhub/services/tracker.py`

### What to build
`TrackerService` manages tracked repositories — add, remove, discover owned repos, exclude/include.

### Class: `TrackerService(db: Database, provider: GitProvider)`

### Methods
- `discover_owned_repos(username: str) -> list[TrackedRepo]` — fetches user repos via GitProvider, inserts new ones as tracking_mode="owned", skips already-tracked repos, respects excluded flag. On each call, discovers newly created repos.
- `track_repo(owner: str, repo: str, mode: TrackingMode = TrackingMode.watched, depth: int = 1) -> TrackedRepo` — adds repo to tracking. Fetches repo info from GitHub to populate metadata. Raises if already tracked.
- `untrack_repo(owner: str, repo: str) -> None` — removes from tracking (cascades delete forks, signals via FK)
- `exclude_repo(repo_name: str) -> None` — sets excluded=True on an owned repo
- `include_repo(repo_name: str) -> None` — sets excluded=False
- `list_tracked_repos(mode: TrackingMode | None = None) -> list[TrackedRepo]` — filtered list, excludes excluded repos unless specifically requested
- `detect_upstream_repos(username: str) -> list[TrackedRepo]` — finds repos the user has forked (is_fork=True in their repo list), gets the parent repo info, tracks the parent with mode="upstream"

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_tracker.py`)
Use real in-memory SQLite + a stub GitProvider (a real class that implements the Protocol with canned data, not a mock framework):
- Test discover_owned_repos adds new repos, returns them
- Test discover_owned_repos skips already-tracked repos on second call
- Test discover_owned_repos respects excluded flag
- Test track_repo adds a watched repo with correct metadata
- Test track_repo raises on duplicate
- Test untrack_repo removes repo and cascaded data
- Test exclude/include toggling
- Test list_tracked_repos filters by mode
- Test detect_upstream_repos identifies forked repos and tracks their parents

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 2 (GitHub provider, database)
title: Tracker service (repo discovery and management)

## Wave 3 — Services (parallelizable with 3.1, 3.4)

### File: `src/forkhub/services/cluster.py`

### What to build
`ClusterService` detects when multiple forks make similar changes independently using vector similarity on signal embeddings.

### Class: `ClusterService(db: Database, embedding_provider: EmbeddingProvider)`

### Methods
- `update_clusters(repo_id: str) -> list[Cluster]` — main entry point:
  1. Get all signals for this repo that don't yet have embeddings in vec_signals
  2. Generate embeddings via `embedding_provider.embed([signal.summary for signal in unembedded])`
  3. Store embeddings in vec_signals table via `db.save_signal_embedding()`
  4. For each recent signal (created in last sync), search for similar signals from DIFFERENT forks via `db.search_similar_signals()`
  5. If distance < threshold (default 0.3) and signals are from different forks, create or update a cluster
  6. When updating existing cluster: add the new signal as a member, increment fork_count if new fork, update description
  7. Return new or updated clusters
- `get_clusters(repo_id: str, min_size: int = 2) -> list[Cluster]` — retrieve existing clusters filtered by minimum fork count
- `_should_cluster(signal_a: Signal, signal_b: Signal, distance: float, threshold: float = 0.3) -> bool`:
  - Must be from different forks (signal_a.fork_id != signal_b.fork_id)
  - Distance must be below threshold
  - Should have overlapping files_involved (at least one common file path or directory)
- `_generate_cluster_label(signals: list[Signal]) -> str` — derive a human-readable label from the common files/categories

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_clusters.py`)
Use real in-memory SQLite + real LocalEmbeddingProvider (per project rules: no mocks):
- Test embedding generation and storage in vec_signals
- Test similar signals from different forks form a cluster
- Test similar signals from the SAME fork do NOT cluster
- Test cluster growth: new signal joins existing cluster, fork_count updates
- Test distance threshold filtering: far signals don't cluster
- Test _should_cluster with overlapping vs non-overlapping files
- Test get_clusters respects min_size filter
- Test cluster label generation

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 2 (database with vec_signals, embedding provider)
title: Cluster detection service (vector similarity)

description:
## Wave 4 — Agent Layer (parallelizable with 4.2)

### Files
- `src/forkhub/agent/tools.py` — custom tool definitions
- `src/forkhub/agent/prompts.py` — system prompts as constants

### What to build: Custom Tools

7 tools using the `@tool` decorator from `claude-agent-sdk`. Each tool wraps service/provider calls and needs access to Database and GitProvider (use module-level or closure-based dependency injection).

**Tools:**

1. `list_forks(owner: str, repo: str, page: int = 1, only_active: bool = True) -> dict`
   - Wraps GitProvider.get_forks(), filters to active forks if only_active
   - Returns dict with forks list and pagination info

2. `get_fork_summary(fork_full_name: str) -> dict`
   - Composite call: get commits ahead/behind, changed file list, recent commit messages
   - Uses GitProvider.compare() and GitProvider.get_commit_messages()
   - This is the CHEAP operation the agent should call first

3. `get_file_diff(fork_full_name: str, file_path: str) -> str`
   - Full diff for one specific file in a fork vs upstream
   - Uses GitProvider.get_file_diff()
   - This is the EXPENSIVE operation the agent calls selectively

4. `get_releases(owner: str, repo: str, since_days: int = 30) -> list[dict]`
   - Wraps GitProvider.get_releases() with date filtering

5. `get_fork_stars(fork_full_name: str) -> dict`
   - Returns star count + velocity (stars gained since last check)
   - Reads from DB fork record

6. `store_signal(fork_full_name: str, category: str, summary: str, significance: int, files_involved: list[str], detail: str | None = None) -> dict`
   - Persists a classified signal to the database
   - Validates category is a valid SignalCategory
   - Returns the created signal ID

7. `search_similar_signals(summary_text: str, limit: int = 5) -> list[dict]`
   - Generates embedding for summary_text, searches vec_signals
   - Returns similar signals with distances for cluster detection

All tools must:
- Return dicts (not Pydantic models) for Agent SDK serialization
- Return `{"error": "message"}` with `is_error: True` on failure, never raise exceptions
- Have clear docstrings that guide the agent on when/how to use them

### What to build: Prompts

`prompts.py` contains system prompts as string constants:

- `COORDINATOR_PROMPT` — "You are a fork analysis agent. Your job is to understand what's happening across the fork constellation..." Includes strategy guidance (skim first, deep-dive selectively).
- `DIFF_ANALYST_PROMPT` — "You are a fork analyst. Given a fork's summary, decide which changes are meaningful..." Strategy: start with get_fork_summary, look at commits for intent, use get_file_diff selectively, call store_signal for findings.
- `DIGEST_WRITER_PROMPT` — "You are a technical writer composing a fork activity digest..." Guidelines: lead with significant items, group by repo, highlight clusters, be concise.

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_agent_tools.py`)
Use real in-memory SQLite + stub GitProvider:
- Test each tool function independently
- Test list_forks with only_active filtering
- Test get_fork_summary returns correct composite data
- Test get_file_diff returns diff string
- Test store_signal persists correctly, returns signal ID
- Test store_signal validates category (rejects invalid)
- Test search_similar_signals returns results with distances
- Test error handling: tools return error dict on failure

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 3 (services, database with data)
title: Agent SDK tools and prompts

close_reason: Analyzer re-amplification bounded: NULL-baseline fork with persistent SHA-fetch failure now gates changed_forks on real divergence evidence (SHA move / pushed_at change / commits_ahead shift) so it's analyzed at most once, not every sync. False 'self-extinguishing' docstring corrected. API-call residual tracked in forkhub-lgh. PR #16 (feature/zaa-bound-null-baseline-analyzer).
description: Code review of 58beb0f: when a fork's stored head_sha is NULL and get_head_sha fails transiently while compare succeeds on the same branch (differential failure, e.g. rate-limited SHA fetch), needs_baseline stays True forever — the fork is re-compared AND re-sent to analyzer.analyze() (real Claude spend) on EVERY sync. Common-case failure (branch gone) fails compare too, so no analyzer cost, but still 2 wasted API calls/sync forever. Docstring claims 'self-extinguishing' — only true when the fetch eventually succeeds. Fix options: per-fork failure-count cap before giving up on baselining; or only append to changed_forks on the needs_baseline path when ahead_by changed vs stored; or persist a baseline_attempts counter. Existing test test_sync.py:935-957 pins single-sync fail-open behavior but not multi-sync amplification — add a two-sync test.
title: NULL-baseline catch-up is unbounded: persistent SHA-fetch failure pins a fork to the analyzer every sync

## Wave 3 — Services (parallelizable with 3.1, 3.3)

### File: `src/forkhub/services/digest.py`

### What to build
`DigestService` generates and delivers periodic digest notifications from accumulated signals.

### Class: `DigestService(db: Database, backends: list[NotificationBackend])`

### Methods
- `generate_digest(config: DigestConfig, since: datetime | None = None) -> Digest`:
  1. Determine time range: `since` override or last digest's created_at for this config
  2. Query all signals in the time range via `db.get_signals_since()`
  3. Filter by config criteria:
     - `min_significance`: exclude signals below threshold
     - `categories`: only include specified categories (None = all)
     - `file_patterns`: only include signals whose files_involved match any glob pattern (None = all)
  4. Group signals by tracked_repo_id
  5. Within each repo group, separate into sections: owned repos, upstream signals (is_upstream=True), watched repos
  6. Get cluster info for each repo via `db.get_clusters_for_repo()`
  7. Build digest title (e.g., "ForkHub Weekly Digest — March 1, 2026")
  8. Build digest body using the grouped signals and clusters
  9. Save digest to DB, return it
- `deliver_digest(digest: Digest) -> list[DeliveryResult]`:
  - Call `backend.deliver(digest)` for each backend
  - Collect all DeliveryResults
  - Update digest.delivered_at in DB
- `generate_and_deliver(config: DigestConfig | None = None, since: datetime | None = None) -> tuple[Digest, list[DeliveryResult]]` — convenience method. If config is None, use global digest config.
- `_matches_file_patterns(signal_files: list[str], patterns: list[str]) -> bool` — glob matching using `fnmatch`

### TDD Tests (`tests/test_digest.py`)
Use real in-memory SQLite + a stub NotificationBackend:
- Test digest generation with various signal combinations
- Test significance filtering: below-threshold signals excluded
- Test category filtering: only matching categories included
- Test file pattern filtering with glob matching
- Test grouping by repo and section (owned vs upstream vs watched)
- Test cluster info included in digest
- Test delivery dispatches to all backends and collects results
- Test deliver updates delivered_at timestamp
- Test empty digest when no signals since last digest
- Test generate_and_deliver convenience method
- Test time range: respects since parameter vs last digest date

### Dependencies
- Blocked by: Wave 2 (database, notification backends)
title: Digest service (generation and delivery)
